_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Copiah County, MS. Formed in 1823 from Hinds County. Hazlehurst is the county seat. Copiah County occupied the transitional zone between the wealthy river counties and the piney woods interior. The county's name derives from a Choctaw word, reflecting the recent displacement of the Choctaw Nation when the county was organized. Records survive well from the 1820s forward.

current_courthouse.location: Hazlehurst, MS — Main Street (rebuilt after fire)

previous_courthouses.location: Gallatin (original county seat) — small frame courthouse
previous_courthouses.fate: County seat moved to Hazlehurst when the New Orleans, Jackson & Great Northern Railroad bypassed Gallatin in the 1850s. Records moved with the county seat.

previous_courthouses.location: Hazlehurst — courthouse on the town square
previous_courthouses.fate: Destroyed by fire in 1901. The fire caused significant record losses. Some volumes were saved, but the destruction was substantial.

records_survived: some marriage records from mid-19th century
record_losses.description: The 1901 courthouse fire in Hazlehurst was one of the more destructive courthouse fires in southwestern Mississippi. Records from the county's first three decades (1823-1850s) suffered the heaviest losses. Later records — particularly those generated after the county seat moved to Hazlehurst and the volume of record-keeping increased — survived in greater proportion. The rebuilding of the courthouse in 1902 occurred quickly, but the lost early records were irrecoverable.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The 1901 fire created a genealogical wall for families in Copiah County before approximately 1850. If {ancestor_name} was in Copiah County in its first decades, the original deeds, wills, and court records may have burned. Federal census schedules, state land office records, and church records become the primary substitutes — and the loss means that some families who were certainly there will never be fully documented from local records.
record_losses.source: Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Guide to Official Records in the Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Jackson, 1994.

deeds.completeness: Incomplete before 1850 due to 1901 fire. Good from mid-19th century onward.
deeds.gaps: Major gap 1823-1850 due to courthouse fire. State land office records at MDAH partially fill the gap for original patent transactions.
deeds.repository: Copiah County Chancery Clerk, Hazlehurst; Mississippi Department of Archives and History
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; limited MDAH digital collections

wills_estates.completeness: Early will books destroyed in 1901 fire. Records from mid-19th century onward survive.
wills_estates.gaps: 1823-1850 estate records largely lost. Some estates may be partially reconstructed from chancery court appeals or from copies held in private family papers.
wills_estates.repository: Copiah County Chancery Clerk; Mississippi Department of Archives and History

marriages.completeness: Partial survival from mid-1840s. Earlier marriage records destroyed in 1901 fire.
marriages.repository: Copiah County Circuit Clerk; Mississippi Department of Archives and History

court_minutes.completeness: Early court minutes lost in fire. Civil War-era and later minutes survive.
court_minutes.repository: Copiah County Circuit Clerk; Mississippi Department of Archives and History

vital_records.completeness: Mississippi statewide registration began 1912. Rural compliance was slow, particularly in the Black community.
vital_records.repository: Mississippi State Department of Health, Vital Records

substitute_sources.description: Original land patents and entries recorded at the state land office survive independently of county records. These document the initial sale of public lands to settlers in Copiah County after the Choctaw cessions — and they were not in the Hazlehurst courthouse when it burned.
substitute_sources.repository: Mississippi Secretary of State, Land Office; Mississippi Department of Archives and History; Bureau of Land Management General Land Office records (online)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for the 1823-1850 gap. Land patents document who purchased land directly from the government after the Choctaw cession opened the area to white settlement. The BLM General Land Office records are fully digitized and searchable online.
substitute_sources.source: Bureau of Land Management. General Land Office Records. https://glorecords.blm.gov.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules from 1830 (the first census after Copiah County's formation) through 1940. These records were held in Washington, not Hazlehurst, and survived the fire.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: For the 1823-1850 period when county records are lost, census records are the primary surviving documentation of who lived in Copiah County. Slave schedules (1850, 1860) document the enslaved population.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Copiah County, MS.

substitute_sources.description: The land that became Copiah County was ceded by the Choctaw Nation in the Treaty of Doak's Stand (1820). Federal records related to the cession, including treaty documents and the subsequent public land survey, document the transition from Choctaw to American sovereignty.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives (RG 75, Bureau of Indian Affairs); Mississippi Department of Archives and History
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: These records document the land's previous occupants and the legal framework under which the first white settlers acquired title. For Choctaw descendants, the treaty records and removal-era documentation are essential.
substitute_sources.source: DeRosier, Arthur H., Jr. The Removal of the Choctaw Indians. Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 1970.

narrative_hooks: The 1901 fire in Hazlehurst burned what the first three decades of Copiah County had produced — the deeds, the wills, the court records that would have documented {ancestor_name}'s earliest years in the county. The federal census and the state land office survived because they were stored somewhere else. The courthouse did not.

Copiah County sits on Choctaw land ceded in 1820. When {ancestor_name} arrived in the 1820s or 1830s, the treaty ink was barely dry and the Choctaw people were being forced west on a road of suffering. The land {pronoun_subject} farmed was someone else's homeland within living memory.

The railroad bypassed Gallatin and the county seat moved to Hazlehurst — and with it, the records. If {ancestor_name} did business at the old courthouse in Gallatin, those papers traveled to a new building and then burned in 1901. The county seat move and the fire are a one-two punch of record disruption.

For the years before 1850 in Copiah County, the genealogist is working without a net — the courthouse records are gone, and the family's story depends on federal census schedules, land office patents, and whatever the family itself preserved.
source_references: Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Guide to Official Records in the Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Jackson, 1994.
Bureau of Land Management. General Land Office Records. https://glorecords.blm.gov.
DeRosier, Arthur H., Jr. The Removal of the Choctaw Indians. Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 1970.
National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Copiah County, MS.
